chunk_summary: This section explores the pathophysiology of wasting in moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) and its clinical implications, focusing on feeding strategies and supplementary interventions to address nutritional deficits.
learning_objectives: Understand the pathophysiological mechanisms underlying wasting in moderate acute malnutrition (MAM).
Identify clinical correlates and diagnostic criteria for MAM.
Evaluate supplementary feeding strategies for managing MAM.
Analyze the impact of nutritional interventions on recovery outcomes.
key_facts: Moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) affects children with a weight-for-height Z-score between -2 and -3 SD.
MAM is associated with increased risk of infections and delayed recovery from illness.
Supplementary feeding programs aim to provide 500-1000 kcal/day to address energy deficits.
Protein intake in MAM management should meet 10-15% of total caloric needs.
Micronutrient supplementation, including zinc and vitamin A, is critical for immune function and recovery.
reference_quote: Supplementary feeding programs are essential in addressing moderate acute malnutrition, providing targeted energy and nutrient support to prevent progression to severe malnutrition.
